topicArea: Rhetoric, the Suspicion of Eloquence & the Impossibility of Unpersuasive Speech

questions.passage:
The complaint is nearly as old as the art it targets. Wherever the teaching of persuasion has flourished, in the assemblies of antiquity, the law courts of every era, the seminar rooms and consulting firms of the present, someone has risen to observe that the trained speaker can make the worse cause appear the better, and that a skill for sale to any client is a menace to the community that must listen to it. The philosophers who first pressed the charge did not deny that eloquence worked; that was precisely the accusation. A power that operates on audiences regardless of where the truth lies, they argued, is not a branch of knowledge but a knack of flattery, cookery for the soul, and its teachers were selling the community's undoing dressed as an education.

The rhetoricians' classic defense was ready almost immediately, and it has scarcely changed since. Rhetoric, they answered, is an instrument, and instruments are innocent of their uses. Speech that moves audiences can serve the honest advocate as readily as the fraud, and it had better, because truth does not argue its own case. In any forum where decisions are made by hearers, the unadorned true account competes against adorned falsehoods, and if the truthful disarm on principle while the unscrupulous train, the principled have not purified the contest but conceded it. On this view the remedy for dangerous eloquence is more eloquence, better distributed, and the teacher of rhetoric is no more culpable for demagogues than the fencing master is for duels.

The neutrality defense is not wrong so much as incomplete, and its incompleteness is structural. Instruments are never entirely indifferent to their uses: each makes some actions easier and others harder, and the forms of persuasive speech are no exception. The devices that move audiences most reliably, the vivid particular case, the memorable phrase, the arc of narrative, the appeal to what the audience already reveres, favor claims that can be made vivid, memorable, narratable, and congenial. A statistical truth, a complicated causal story, an unwelcome correction, enters the contest of persuasion carrying weight the simple falsehood does not. The scale, in other words, is not level even before the speakers begin, and a defense that treats every misuse of rhetoric as the operator's fault has quietly assumed away the tilt built into the machine.

It might seem to follow that the reformer's task is to strip persuasion out of public speech, to insist on plain statement and let the facts do what they can. The prescription fails on its own terms, because there is no non-rhetorical way to present anything. Plainness is itself a style, with its own persuasive signature, the laboratory report's passive constructions and suppressed agents perform trustworthiness as surely as the orator's periods perform passion, and the choice to sound objective is a choice about how to move an audience, not an abstention from moving one. The dream of speech scrubbed of persuasion mistakes one rhetoric, currently prestigious, for rhetoric's absence, and its dreamers are most dangerous when they believe themselves outside the art, since they alone are practicing it unexamined.

If persuasion can be neither surrendered nor escaped, the remaining question is what can be done with its tilt, and here the record offers a suggestive pattern. The practices most societies trust for finding truth do not banish rhetoric; they oppose it to itself under rules. The trial subjects each advocate's narrative to an adversary paid to dismantle it; peer review sets a claimant's enthusiasm against a referee's professional skepticism; parliamentary procedure rations the floor so that eloquence must at least take turns. None of these arrangements assumes speakers will be fair. They assume the opposite, and are engineered so that the collision of interested performances leaves the audience better informed than any single performance intended. The counterweight to rhetoric, in short, has never been silence or plainness. It has been counter-rhetoric, institutionalized.

The ancient quarrel, on this reading, was never going to be won by either side, because both were right about different halves of a permanent condition. The philosophers were right that persuasion outruns proof, that assent can be manufactured, and that communities are at the mercy of what moves them. The rhetoricians were right that there is no exit, that any community which must decide together must persuade, and that disarming the honest is a gift to no one but their opponents. What neither quite said is that the object of a mature public culture is not the purification of speech but its answerability: forums built so that every performance can be met, every vividness questioned, every tilt of the machine at least partially opposed by an equal and opposite lean. Eloquence unanswered is the menace the philosophers described. The work, never finished, is arranging for the answer.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ancient philosophers were correct that rhetoric is mere flattery, and modern institutions should therefore restrict the teaching of persuasion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Since persuasion can neither be surrendered without conceding to the unscrupulous nor escaped through plain speech, and since rhetorical forms carry a built-in tilt, the mature response is not purifying speech but institutionalizing counter-rhetoric so that every performance can be answered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The neutrality defense fully vindicates rhetoric, since instruments cannot be blamed for their operators' misuses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scientific writing has escaped rhetoric by adopting plain, objective prose that presents facts without persuasion.

explanations.A: This choice awards victory to one side of a quarrel the passage declares unwinnable by either. The final paragraph credits the philosophers with half the truth, persuasion outruns proof, while crediting the rhetoricians with the other half, there is no exit, and the passage proposes answerability rather than restriction as the mature response.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage traces the ancient charge and the neutrality defense, finds the defense structurally incomplete because persuasive forms favor the vivid and congenial over the statistical and unwelcome, shows that plain statement is itself a rhetoric rather than an escape, and concludes that trusted truth-finding practices oppose rhetoric to itself under rules, making institutionalized counter-rhetoric and answerable performance the object of a mature public culture.
explanations.C: This choice endorses the defense at exactly the strength the third paragraph denies it. The passage calls the neutrality defense incomplete rather than wrong, arguing that instruments make some actions easier than others and that treating every misuse as the operator's fault assumes away the tilt built into the machine.
explanations.D: This choice claims the escape the fourth paragraph forecloses. The passage argues that the laboratory report's suppressed agents and passive constructions perform trustworthiness, that sounding objective is a choice about how to move an audience, and that mistaking a prestigious rhetoric for rhetoric's absence is the most dangerous error available.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: foreclose the reformer's apparent remedy by showing that plain statement is itself a style with a persuasive signature, so no presentation stands outside rhetoric.

choices.label: B
choices.text: concede that laboratory reports, unlike orations, succeed in presenting facts without moving their audiences.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recommend that public speech adopt the passive constructions of scientific prose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: introduce the adversarial institutions that the fifth paragraph describes.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph opens with the remedy that might seem to follow from the third paragraph's tilt, strip persuasion out and insist on plain statement, and defeats it on its own terms: plainness is a style whose suppressed agents perform trustworthiness, sounding objective is a way of moving an audience, and those who believe themselves outside the art are practicing it unexamined, so no non-rhetorical presentation exists.
explanations.B: This choice reverses the paragraph's treatment of its central example. The laboratory report appears precisely to show that its conventions perform trustworthiness as surely as the orator's periods perform passion, meaning it moves audiences by other means rather than declining to move them.
explanations.C: This choice converts a diagnostic observation into a stylistic endorsement the paragraph never offers. Scientific prose is analyzed as one rhetoric among others, currently prestigious; nothing recommends extending its conventions to public speech generally.
explanations.D: This choice assigns the paragraph the next paragraph's work. Trials, peer review, and parliamentary procedure enter in the fifth paragraph as the institutional response; the fourth paragraph is occupied with eliminating the plain-speech alternative that would have made such institutions unnecessary.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the rhetoricians' neutrality defense is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wholly dismissive, since the defense is refuted by the existence of demagogues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: fully persuaded, since the fencing-master comparison settles the question of the teacher's culpability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: acknowledging its force while judging it structurally incomplete, since rhetorical forms are not indifferent instruments but favor vivid and congenial claims over complicated and unwelcome ones.

choices.label: D
choices.text: amused, treating the defense as a historical curiosity with no bearing on modern communication.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes qualification for refutation. The passage grants the defense real force, truth does not argue its own case, and disarming the truthful concedes the contest, and the final paragraph affirms the rhetoricians were right that there is no exit, so the defense is limited, not dismissed.
explanations.B: This choice stops reading at the second paragraph. The third paragraph immediately declares the defense not wrong so much as incomplete, and its incompleteness structural, which is not the verdict of a fully persuaded reader; the fencing-master comparison is reported as the defense's own analogy, not endorsed as settling anything.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage states the defense at full strength, then holds that instruments are never entirely indifferent to their uses: the devices that move audiences most reliably favor claims that can be made vivid, memorable, and congenial, so statistical truths and unwelcome corrections enter the contest carrying extra weight, a tilt the defense assumes away by blaming only operators.
explanations.D: This choice imports a condescension the passage never displays. The defense is described as ready almost immediately and scarcely changed since, a remark about its durability, and the passage engages it as a serious living position whose partial truth survives into the final paragraph's verdict.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the trusted truth-finding practices described in the fifth paragraph share which of the following features?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They require participants to renounce persuasive techniques before taking part.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They succeed only when the individuals involved argue in good faith.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They were each designed by the ancient philosophers who first accused rhetoric of flattery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They assume speakers will be partial and are engineered so that opposed interested performances leave audiences better informed than any single performance intended.

explanations.A: This choice describes the purification strategy the passage rejects. The fifth paragraph states that these practices do not banish rhetoric; advocates, claimants, and parliamentarians persuade freely, and the arrangements work by opposing their persuasions rather than by extracting renunciations.
explanations.B: This choice inverts the design principle the paragraph identifies. The passage says none of these arrangements assumes speakers will be fair, and that they assume the opposite, so good faith is precisely what the institutions are built not to depend upon.
explanations.C: This choice invents a lineage the passage never asserts. The trial, peer review, and parliamentary procedure are offered as a pattern in the practices societies trust, with no claim that the philosophers who charged rhetoric with flattery designed any of them.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph describes the trial's paid adversary, peer review's professional skeptic, and procedure's rationed floor as arrangements that oppose rhetoric to itself under rules, assuming partiality and engineering the collision of interested performances so that the audience ends better informed than any single performer intended, counter-rhetoric institutionalized.

questions.stem: A public health agency, seeking credibility, announces that its communications will henceforth contain no persuasive elements, only neutral presentation of data in unembellished language. Based on the fourth paragraph, the author would most likely observe that the agency:

choices.label: A
choices.text: has correctly identified the only reliable path to public trust.

choices.label: B
choices.text: has adopted a persuasive style while declaring itself outside persuasion, the posture the passage calls most dangerous because it is practiced unexamined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: should instead hire trained orators to compete with misinformation on equal terms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: has violated the adversarial norms that govern peer review.

explanations.A: This choice endorses the plain-speech remedy the fourth paragraph defeats on its own terms. Unembellished presentation is itself a style with a persuasive signature, so the announced escape from persuasion is not available, whatever its effect on trust.
explanations.B: Correct. The agency's neutral, unembellished presentation is exactly the plainness the fourth paragraph analyzes as a style that performs trustworthiness, and its announcement that it stands outside persuasion matches the passage's warning that the dreamers of scrubbed speech are most dangerous when they believe themselves outside the art, since they alone are practicing it unexamined.
explanations.C: This choice reflects the second paragraph's remedy of more eloquence better distributed, which is the rhetoricians' classic position rather than the fourth paragraph's point. The question asks what the fourth paragraph implies about the agency's self-description, which concerns the impossibility of non-rhetorical presentation, not staffing strategy.
explanations.D: This choice misapplies an institution from the fifth paragraph. Peer review's adversarial norms govern the checking of claims among professionals; an agency's public communication style neither invokes nor violates them, and the relevant analysis is the fourth paragraph's treatment of objectivity as a rhetorical choice.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the third paragraph's claim that persuasive forms carry a built-in tilt favoring vivid, simple claims over statistical and complicated ones?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that audiences enjoy vivid anecdotes more than they enjoy statistical presentations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that some demagogues have occasionally been defeated in open debate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A large body of experimental research finding that when complicated causal accounts and statistical corrections are presented with ordinary competence, audiences find them as persuasive and as memorable as vivid particular cases making opposing claims, with no systematic advantage for simplicity or vividness across formats and topics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that training in statistics is increasingly common among journalists.

explanations.A: This choice supports rather than weakens the tilt claim. Audiences' greater enjoyment of vivid anecdotes is one plausible mechanism behind the asymmetry the paragraph describes, so the finding leans in the passage's direction.
explanations.B: This choice records occasional defeats, which the tilt claim fully accommodates. A scale that is not level can still see the disadvantaged side win sometimes; the paragraph asserts systematic extra weight, not invincibility for the vivid and simple.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph claims a structural asymmetry: statistical truths and complicated stories enter the contest carrying weight simple falsehoods do not. Broad experimental evidence that competently presented complications and corrections persuade and stick as well as vivid particulars, with no systematic advantage for simplicity across formats and topics, would directly contradict the claimed tilt in the machine.
explanations.D: This choice describes a change in communicators' training, which might eventually affect how claims are presented but does not test whether the forms themselves favor vivid over complicated content, the structural claim at issue.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fifth paragraph's claim that opposing rhetoric to itself under rules leaves audiences better informed than any single performance intended?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studies across trial, review, and deliberative settings finding that decision-makers exposed to competing advocated presentations under structured turn-taking and adversarial questioning reached more accurate conclusions, and identified more of each side's omissions, than matched decision-makers who heard only one polished presentation, even when the single presentation was the more accurate one available.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that jurors report finding trials more engaging than written summaries of the same evidence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that peer reviewers spend more hours per manuscript than they did in earlier decades.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that parliamentary debates are widely televised and attract substantial audiences.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim is that engineered collisions of interested performances inform audiences beyond any single performer's intention. Cross-setting studies showing that structured adversarial exposure produced more accurate conclusions and better detection of each side's omissions than even the best single presentation directly demonstrate the informational surplus the paragraph attributes to institutionalized counter-rhetoric.
explanations.B: This choice measures engagement, not information. Jurors finding trials absorbing says nothing about whether adversarial structure leaves them better informed or more accurate than alternatives, which is the specific benefit claimed.
explanations.C: This choice tracks reviewer effort over time, an input measure that does not show whether the adversarial pairing of claimant enthusiasm against professional skepticism improves what audiences of the process, editors, readers, ultimately know.
explanations.D: This choice documents the reach of one institution's broadcasts, which concerns how many people watch rather than whether the rule-governed collision of performances informs them better than single performances would.
